title: Exercises: Measurement Error Mitigation
description: Hands-on practice with measurement error mitigation

exercises.title: Build Confusion Matrix
exercises.description: Build a confusion matrix from calibration data and verify its properties.
exercises.starterCode:

# Test data
calibration = {
    '0': {'0': 9700, '1': 300},
    '1': {'0': 500, '1': 9500}

M = build_confusion_matrix(calibration)
print(f"Confusion matrix:\n{M}")

# Verify: columns should sum to 1

exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def build_confusion_matrix(calibration_data):
    """Build confusion matrix from calibration measurements."""
    n_states = len(calibration_data)
    M = np.zeros((n_states, n_states))
    
    for prep_state, counts in calibration_data.items():
        prep_idx = int(prep_state, 2)
        total = sum(counts.values())
        
        for meas_state, count in counts.items():
            meas_idx = int(meas_state, 2)
            M[meas_idx, prep_idx] = count / total
    
    return M

# Test
calibration = {
    '0': {'0': 9700, '1': 300},
    '1': {'0': 500, '1': 9500}
}

M = build_confusion_matrix(calibration)
print(f"Confusion matrix:\n{M}")
print(f"\nColumn sums: {M.sum(axis=0)}")
print(f"P(1|0) = {M[1,0]:.2%}")
print(f"P(0|1) = {M[0,1]:.2%}")
hints: Convert binary strings to indices using int(str, 2)

exercises.title: Matrix Inversion Mitigation

    # 2. Apply to noisy probs

    # 4. Normalize

# Test
M = np.array([[0.97, 0.05], [0.03, 0.95]])
noisy = np.array([0.4, 0.6])

mitigated = mitigate_probabilities(noisy, M)
print(f"Mitigated: {mitigated}")
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def mitigate_probabilities(noisy_probs, confusion_matrix):
    """Mitigate measurement errors."""
    # Invert confusion matrix
    try:
        inv_M = np.linalg.inv(confusion_matrix)
    except np.linalg.LinAlgError:
        inv_M = np.linalg.pinv(confusion_matrix)
    
    # Apply to noisy probabilities
    mitigated = inv_M @ noisy_probs
    
    # Clip negative values
    mitigated = np.clip(mitigated, 0, None)
    
    # Renormalize
    total = np.sum(mitigated)
    if total > 0:
        mitigated = mitigated / total
    
    return mitigated

# Test
M = np.array([[0.97, 0.05], [0.03, 0.95]])
noisy = np.array([0.4, 0.6])

mitigated = mitigate_probabilities(noisy, M)

print(f"Confusion matrix:\n{M}")
print(f"\nNoisy probs: {noisy}")
print(f"Mitigated: {mitigated}")
print(f"Sum: {mitigated.sum():.4f}")
print(f"All non-negative: {np.all(mitigated >= 0)}")
hints: Use np.linalg.inv or pinv for inversion

exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

class TensoredMitigator:
    def __init__(self, qubit_matrices):
        self.n_qubits = len(qubit_matrices)
        
        # Build full matrix via tensor product
        self.full_matrix = qubit_matrices[0]
        for M in qubit_matrices[1:]:
            self.full_matrix = np.kron(self.full_matrix, M)
        
        # Compute inverse
        self.inverse = np.linalg.pinv(self.full_matrix)
    
    def mitigate(self, noisy_probs):
        mitigated = self.inverse @ noisy_probs
        mitigated = np.clip(mitigated, 0, None)
        return mitigated / np.sum(mitigated)

# Test
M0 = np.array([[0.98, 0.04], [0.02, 0.96]])
M1 = np.array([[0.97, 0.05], [0.03, 0.95]])

mitigator = TensoredMitigator([M0, M1])

print(f"Full matrix shape: {mitigator.full_matrix.shape}")
print(f"Full matrix:\n{mitigator.full_matrix}")

# Bell state measurement
ideal = np.array([0.5, 0.0, 0.0, 0.5])
noisy = mitigator.full_matrix @ ideal
mitigated = mitigator.mitigate(noisy)

print(f"\nIdeal: {ideal}")
print(f"Noisy: {noisy}")
print(f"Mitigated: {mitigated}")
hints: Build full matrix iteratively from single-qubit matrices
Same mitigation logic as single qubit
